scenario: A form has a Country selector with 200 options. Designer wonders if to use Select dropdown or Radio buttons.
question: Form Select vs Radio buttons?

options.text: Radio buttons are most appropriate for small option sets of 2-5 items where all choices should be visible simultaneously. Displaying 200 countries as radio buttons would create an unusable, scrolling list. md:17. It provides a compact picker with type-ahead filtering.

Radio buttons and Select dropdown have significantly different user experiences for large option lists. Radio buttons render all options as visible list items simultaneously, which becomes unwieldy with more than 5-7 options. Select dropdown renders as a single compact element that expands when clicked, and with the Searchable property enabled it allows type-ahead filtering. For 200 countries, the user experience difference between the two components is substantial -- they are not interchangeable.

A Text Field allows free-form user input and provides no constraint to a predefined set of values. For a country selection, this means users could enter misspellings, abbreviations, or invalid country names that downstream logic cannot process reliably. Providing a constrained selection from the 200 valid countries requires a component that presents those options explicitly, either as radio buttons (impractical at 200 items) or as a searchable Select dropdown that enforces valid choices from a defined list.

Radio buttons render all 200 options simultaneously and are only recommended for 2-5 choices. The Select component's values can be populated from a static list, a FEEL expression evaluating a process variable, or a dynamic data source.

a.text: Incorrect. Form component selection per use case: Radio buttons: Visual: list of options, all visible, one selected. Best for: 2-5 options (yes/no, small enumerations). Pros: instant scan; visible alternatives. Cons: takes vertical space; impractical for many options. Select dropdown: Visual: collapsed; expands on click; one option visible at a time. Best for: 5+ options. Pros: compact; scalable. Cons: requires interaction to see options. Searchable Select / Autocomplete (verify availability): Combines Select with search field. Best for: very long lists (countries, currencies, products). Pros: fast filtering; keyboard-friendly. Cons: more complex; component may need configuration. Checkbox group (for multi-select): Multiple selections allowed. Visual similar to radio but with checkmarks. Multi-select dropdown: Compact multi-select. For many options with multiple choices. For 200-country list: Searchable Select: ideal UX (type "ger" → see Germany). Plain Select: works but slow to scroll. Radio: terrible UX (200 buttons). Decision matrix: | Options count | Single-select | Multi-select | |---------------|---------------|--------------| | 2-5 | Radio | Checkbox group | | 5-20 | Select | Multi-select dropdown | | 20+ | Searchable Select | Multi-select with search | Accessibility considerations: Keyboard navigation: all components should support. Screen readers: ensure labels properly associated. High contrast: visual indicators clear. Data binding: Select / Radio: bound to a single variable (the selected value). Multi-select / Checkbox group: bound to a list variable. Option sources: Static: hardcoded in form definition. Dynamic: from process variable (e.g., countries list passed in). External: fetch from API (component-dependent).
